elements.sent-2411-001: Yanlong Wen is the corresponding author.
elements.sent-2411-002: Graph generation is one of the most challenging tasks in recent years, and its core is to learn the ground truth distribution hiding in the training data.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, training data may not be available due to security concerns or unaffordable costs, which severely blows the learning models, especially the deep generative models.
elements.sent-2411-004: The dilemma leads us to rethink non-learned generation methods based on graph invariant features.
elements.sent-2411-005: Based on the observation of scale-free property, we propose a hierarchical Poisson graph generation algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-006: Specifically, we design a two-stage generation strategy.
elements.sent-2411-007: In the first stage, we sample multiple anchor nodes according to the Poisson distribution to further guide the formation of substructures, splitting the initial node set into multiple ones.
elements.sent-2411-008: Next, we progressively generate edges by sampling nodes through a degree mixing distribution, adjusting the tolerance towards exotic structures via two thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-009: We provide theoretical guarantees for hierarchical generation and verify the effectiveness of our method under 12 datasets of three categories.
elements.sent-2411-010: Experimental results show that our method fits the ground truth distribution better than various generation strategies and other distribution observations.
elements.sent-2411-011: Graph generation task, widely used for scenarios like social analysis~\cite{ref:social2021,ref:social2022,ref:social2024 , molecule generation~\cite{ref:bio2019,ref:bio2023,ref:bio2024 , and structure modeling~\cite{ref:ss2022,ref:ss2023,ref:ss2024 , is one of the hottest researches in recent years.}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Maintaining the distribution similarity between the generated and ground truth graphs is the core and one of the challenges of this task.
elements.sent-2411-013: Deep Generative Models (DGM) are the mainstream solutions nowadays~\cite{ref:DGM,ref:CF,ref:GraphRNN,ref:GraphARM,ref:GraphILE .}
elements.sent-2411-014: DGM learns the latent distribution features hiding in the training data, generating graphs closer to the requirements.
elements.sent-2411-015: However, training data may not be available due to security concerns or high data acquisition costs~\cite{ref:trans2024 , striking learning models severely, especially DGM.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Only plain graph property data, such as the number of nodes and edges, are available when we limit the training data.
elements.sent-2411-018: The learnable distribution space of DGM switches from the training set to the search space of node permutations, resulting in the generation of mess graphs out of distribution (in red).
elements.sent-2411-019: Some studies have proposed solutions to this problem, such as graph transfer learning~\cite{ref:trans2019,ref:trans2023,ref:trans2024 , which realizes graph generation in restricted scenarios by learning invariant features in other training data that can be easily obtained.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Nevertheless, the quality of such solutions depends heavily on the quality of the training data before transfer.
elements.sent-2411-021: There is heterogeneity in the data distribution of different scenarios~\cite{ref:DS .}
elements.sent-2411-022: The model performance is unsatisfactory if there are considerable differences in the invariant features.
elements.sent-2411-023: For example, social networks have many clusters, while molecules rely on functional groups with unique semantics.
elements.sent-2411-024: Models can not learn sufficient invariant features transferring one scene above to another.
elements.sent-2411-025: Improving the quality of training data further increases the acquisition cost, returning to the previous limitations.

However, with invariant features dragging from observation on universal data available, we can generate graphs fitting ground truth distribution well without learning (in blue).} \label{fig:pre}
elements.sent-2411-026: This dilemma urges us to rethink non-learned generation methods based on graph invariant features.
elements.sent-2411-027: Throughout the history of graph generation tasks, traditional methods have been designed on universal observations maintained in real-world scenarios, such as randomness~\cite{ref:ER , small world~\cite{ref:WS , scale-free~\cite{ref:BA,ref:CL,ref:RMAT , etc. These observations are equal to invariant features independent of the training data, satisfying the properties of graphs under most scenarios.}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Among them, we pay attention to the scale-free property of graphs.
elements.sent-2411-029: Scale-free, also known as power-law, heavy-tailed, etc., refers to a phenomenon in which a small number of nodes in networks have a large number of connections, while most nodes have only a few.
elements.sent-2411-030: Many systems in the real world have this property~\cite{ref:survey2022,ref:BA , reflected in the distribution of various aspects, such as degree, label, resources, etc. It will serve as a significant invariant feature observation that effectively organizes the basic graph information to direct the generation.}
elements.sent-2411-031: But, instead of DGM learning the real-world distribution accurately, a selected invariant feature often orients to a class of graph sets with its hand-crafted characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-032: For example, Chung-Lu (CL)~\cite{ref:CL generates strictly scale-free networks and does not perform well in generating other graphs that do not follow this degree distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-033: R-MAT~\cite{ref:RMAT , a scale-free graph generator based on an iterative strategy, creates graphs primarily depending on the initial graph.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Some more methods~\cite{ref:BTER,ref:Darwini,ref:MMSB serve the corresponding set of graphs depending on the user-defined feature constraints, while others~\cite{ref:gMark,ref:LinkBench,ref:LinkGen define particular scenarios, leaving the decision to randomness.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: When learning approaches are limited as a resource, the unapproachable power of predefined features kills the generalization ability of non-learned methods~\cite{ref:survey2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Therefore, whether enhancing the generality of invariant features or weakening selected features by noises, restrictions, etc., can improve the support of non-learned methods for rich graph categories.
elements.sent-2411-037: We further reinforce the limited resources problem setting with real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-038: On the one hand, the training data may be constrained.
elements.sent-2411-039: For example, users' relationships have semantics~\cite{ref:seq2022 (e.g., motifs~\cite{ref:motif ).}}
elements.sent-2411-040: In this case, it is not enough to provide basic data anonymization.
elements.sent-2411-041: The implicit distribution patterns are leaked if the training data is available, causing severe security problems.
elements.sent-2411-042: On the other hand, learning approaches may be limited.
elements.sent-2411-043: For example, there is insufficient data and resources with quantity and quality assurance in some marginal application scenarios to support model learning.
elements.sent-2411-044: In addition, due to the black-box nature of the learning model, the learned invariant features do not have good interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-045: Admittedly, generating graphs relying entirely on basic information such as the number of nodes and edges is tantamount to randomly selecting from the search space.
elements.sent-2411-046: DGM mainly obtains real-world distribution through feature matrix~\cite{ref:adj2020,ref:adj2021,ref:adj2022,ref:adj2023 , node sequence~\cite{ref:seq2022,ref:seq2024 , etc., while some extend the sampling space by mixup through the semantic information of specific scenes~\cite{ref:seman2022,ref:seman2023,ref:seman2024 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: These models are training data available, and the ground truth distribution is known.
elements.sent-2411-048: How to use as little additional information as possible without breaking the limited resources is also a vital consideration in designing a generation strategy for universal graphs.
elements.sent-2411-049: For example, Erd\H{o s-R\'enyi (ER)~\cite{ref:ER , the most typical random graph model, only employs the number of nodes and a uniform probability of edge generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Barab\'asi-Albert (BA)~\cite{ref:BA gradually generates the edges in the graph based on the preferential attachment, including three parameters: the initial graph, the number of nodes, and the number of coming edges for new nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Overall, to devise graph generation methods under limited resources scenarios, we have the following challenges: (1) Capture powerful invariant features.
elements.sent-2411-052: The ground truth distribution cannot be learned directly due to the difficulty of obtaining training data.
elements.sent-2411-053: We need a generation strategy based on a powerful invariant feature covering the majority of scenes.
elements.sent-2411-054: Traditional methods are engineered for a family of graphs with selected features, leading to poor scalability~\cite{ref:survey2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-055: We need to weaken the influence of particular features to support the generation of diverse structures.
elements.sent-2411-056: The effect of invariant feature selection will eventually be reflected in the distance evaluation between the generated and real-world graphs.
elements.sent-2411-057: We need to generate graphs closer to the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-058: To solve the above problems, we propose a hierarchical Poisson graph generation algorithm, which generates universal graphs under the observation of the scale-free property.
elements.sent-2411-059: In the first stage, we sample a set of anchor nodes based on the Poisson distribution to further guide the formation of substructures, splitting the node set into multiple ones.
elements.sent-2411-060: It corresponds to challenge 1, where we strictly follow scale-free invariant features for graph generation.
elements.sent-2411-061: In the second stage, we employ preferential attachment to weight the Poisson distribution to alleviate the feature limitations mentioned in challenge 2.
elements.sent-2411-062: We progressively generate the remaining edges by sampling node pairs through a degree mixing distribution.
elements.sent-2411-063: Finally, facing challenge 3, we leverage two basic graph information as thresholds to adjust the algorithm tolerance for generating exotic structures and further enrich the search space well-fitted to ground truth distribution.
elements.sent-2411-064: We provide theoretical underpinning that guarantees that the generative process is interpretable.
elements.sent-2411-065: In summary, our contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-066: We explore the possibility of generating graphs in the setting of limited resources and rethink the generation strategy only through basic graph properties.
elements.sent-2411-067: We design a hierarchical Poisson generator based on the scale-free property for universal graphs under limited resources, which creates graphs closer to the real-world distribution.
elements.sent-2411-068: We provide theoretical guarantees for our method and design a fast version in a pairwise perspective, achieving a balance between performance and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-069: Experiments on 12 datasets in three categories give a thorough perspective to evaluate our work.
elements.sent-2411-070: Results show that our method can cover most scenarios when the ground truth distribution is unknown.
elements.sent-2411-071: In this section, we systematically review the graph generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-072: It has a long development history, briefly separated into traditional methods and deep generative models based on time.
elements.sent-2411-073: Finally, we briefly introduce methods based on transfer learning.
elements.sent-2411-074: In the early stage, traditional graph generation methods rely on human-made characteristics or particular strict constraints.
elements.sent-2411-075: Erd\H{o s-R\'enyi (ER)~\cite{ref:ER and Watts-Strogatz (WS)~\cite{ref:WS are the most famous graph generation paradigms.}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The former is $\mathcal{G _{np}$, where $n$ is the number of nodes and $p$ is the connection probability between every node pair.}
elements.sent-2411-077: The latter is $\mathcal{G (n, k, p)$, which requires that each node associates with its $k$ neighbors at initialization.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Then, one of the endpoints of each edge will change with probability $p$.
elements.sent-2411-079: The graphs created by ER have a low clustering coefficient and do not have the properties of real-world graphs.
elements.sent-2411-080: WS overcomes this and maintains properties like high clustering and small world but may produce unrealistic degree distribution.
elements.sent-2411-081: Barab\'asi-Albert (BA)~\cite{ref:BA pays attention to the preferential attachment phenomenon, defines $p$ as the degree proportion where $p_u = \frac{d_u {\sum_{v \in \mathcal{N (u) d_v}$, and proposes a progressive generation method that gradually adds nodes.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Therefore, BTER~\cite{ref:BTER created a hierarchical generation method combining ER and BA, leveraging the node proportion to connect multiple ER pre-generated graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Similar to hierarchy, another branch of recursive generation exists.

elements.sent-2411-085: R-MAT~\cite{ref:RMAT and Kronecker~\cite{ref:Kronecker notice the adjacency matrix, creating self-similarity graphs by copying the initial one.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Meanwhile, Mixed-Membership Stochastic Block (MMSB)~\cite{ref:MMSB shares the same idea of parsing graphs into multiple blocks as BTER, which builds a reliable community analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-087: In summary, traditional methods can generate graphs within a particular pattern with small amounts of graph statistical parameters, guaranteeing robust interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-088: Considering the limitations of traditional methods, researchers have shifted their attention to Deep Generative Models (DGM) in recent years.
elements.sent-2411-089: Depending on the technique employed, DGM can be divided into four categories: Variational Auto-Encoders (VAE)~\cite{ref:VAE , Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN)~\cite{ref:GAN , Normalizing Flows (NF)~\cite{ref:NF , and Diffusion models~\cite{ref:Diff .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: VAE and NF transform the original graph feature space into a low-dimensional vector, where the former leverages a well-trained Graph Neural Network (GNN) based encoder and decoder, and the latter uses a sequence of invertible functions.
elements.sent-2411-091: GAN implicitly learns the distribution of the original graph by training a pair of generators and discriminators, which play against each other.
elements.sent-2411-092: Denoising diffusion models originated from the field of computer vision.
elements.sent-2411-093: The core idea is to perturb the original distribution through a designed noise model and then train a learnable denoising process to recover the original graph data from the noise.
elements.sent-2411-094: We use an earlier contemporaneous GraphRNN~\cite{ref:GraphRNN , which leverages recurrent neural networks as the backbone, to represent the single class of technical schemes.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Meanwhile, the majority of popular DGM uses a mixed architecture.
elements.sent-2411-096: For example, GraphARM~\cite{ref:GraphARM relies on an autoregressive diffusion scheme to improve the generation speed of a single solution.}
elements.sent-2411-097: GraphILE~\cite{ref:GraphILE designs the pipeline based on graph coarsening and expansion techniques and integrates the denoising process for training.}
elements.sent-2411-098: CatFlow~\cite{ref:CF introduces variational inference to flow matching, amplifying the advantages of both.}
elements.sent-2411-099: In short, DGM learns the probable graph distribution from the training data and then guides the following generation.

elements.sent-2411-365: In this section, we present a comprehensive rationale for the motivation behind our work.
elements.sent-2411-366: First, we address the critical challenges posed by limited resources setting.
elements.sent-2411-367: On one hand, we identify significant limitations in training data availability.
elements.sent-2411-368: For example, users' relationships may have semantics~\cite{ref:seq2022 (e.g., motifs~\cite{ref:motif ).}}
elements.sent-2411-369: In this case, conventional data anonymization techniques are not enough, as the implicit distribution patterns are leaked if the training data is available, causing severe security problems.
elements.sent-2411-370: For example, there are insufficient data and resources with quantity and quality assurance in some marginal application scenarios to support model learning.
elements.sent-2411-371: It leads to the suffering of solutions such as deep generative models, large language models, etc., which require both data and training resources.
elements.sent-2411-372: Furthermore due to the black-box nature of the learning model, the learned invariant features do not have good interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-373: Therefore, we need to discuss graph generation methods back to random graph theories in limited resources scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-374: Next, we elaborate on the details of our method.
elements.sent-2411-375: Since the resources are limited, there is an absence of prior knowledge regarding the true graph distribution, our major task is to capture robust invariant features that can accommodate a broader spectrum of graph categories, compensating for the scarcity of resources available.
elements.sent-2411-376: Thereby, we draw inspiration from the scale-free properties observed in natural networks.
elements.sent-2411-377: However, recognizing that strict reliance on a single characteristic may decay the generalization capability of generated graphs, we introduce a degree mixing strategy in the second stage of our framework.
elements.sent-2411-378: This mechanism allows a weighted scaling based on the scale-free distribution while maintaining flexibility, which mitigates the rigid constraints imposed by single-feature dominance and optimizes the distribution of generated graphs through parameter adjustments.
elements.sent-2411-379: Considering that Poisson distribution represents one of the most prevalent scale-free distributions, we leverage it as our primary sampling base.
elements.sent-2411-380: We justify this selection in the experiment (see Sec $5.5^*$).
elements.sent-2411-381: Note that we use $^*$ to represent the content in the main text.
elements.sent-2411-382: We conduct comprehensive analyses with various distributions, including non-scale-free (e.g., Normal, Uniform) and approximate scale-free (e.g., Exponential, Pareto).
elements.sent-2411-383: The experimental results demonstrate the superior performance of our method.

elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Our Method: Hierarchical Scale-free Graph Generation} \label{alg:alg} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE the number of nodes $N$, the number of edges $M$, the max degree $d_{max}$; \ENSURE the generated graph $\mathcal{G}$; \STATE $\bar{D} \xleftarrow{} \frac{2M}{N}$, $\mathcal{E} \xleftarrow{} \emptyset$; \STATE $\mathcal{G}_{sub} \xleftarrow{} [(d_{max} + 1, d_{max})]$, $sN \xleftarrow{} N - d_{max} - 1$; \WHILE{$sN > 0$} \STATE sample one possible $d \sim P(\bar{D})$; \STATE add a star graph $(d + 1, d)$ to $\mathcal{G}_{sub}$; \STATE $sN \xleftarrow{} sN - d - 1$; \ENDWHILE \STATE build a degree list $L_{ent}$; \FORALL{$\mathcal{G}_{sub}^{(i)} \in \mathcal{G}_{sub}$} \STATE sample a node $u \in \mathcal{V}_{sub}^{(i)}$; \STATE build a probability list $plist$; \STATE sample a node $v \in \mathcal{V}_{sub}^{(j)}, (j \neq i)$ based on $plist$; \STATE add edge $e_{uv}$ to $\mathcal{E}$ and update $L_{ent}$; \ENDFOR \STATE build a graph $\mathcal{G}$ based on $\mathcal{G}_{sub}$ and $\mathcal{E}$; \STATE $\mathcal{E} \xleftarrow{} \emptyset$, $\mathcal{E}' \xleftarrow{} \emptyset$, $sM \xleftarrow{} M - |\mathcal{E}_\mathcal{G}|$; \WHILE{$sM > 0$} \STATE build a probability list $plist$; \STATE sample a pair $u, v$ based on $plist$; \STATE add edge $e_{uv}$ to $\mathcal{E}$ and update $L_{ent}$; \STATE $sM \xleftarrow{} sM - |\mathcal{E}|$, $\mathcal{E}' \xleftarrow{} \mathcal{E}' \cup \mathcal{E}$, $\mathcal{E} \xleftarrow{} \emptyset$; \ENDWHILE \STATE add edges in $\mathcal{E}'$ to $\mathcal{G}$; \STATE return $\mathcal{G}$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-399: In recent years, there has been a growing trend of exploring the utilization of Large Language Models (LLMs) or agents for generating scale-free networks.

elements.sent-2411-401: However, considering that the powerful tools such as ChatGPT and DeepSeek are available and convenient to integrate, we conduct some experiments between our method and LLM under the neglect of potential data leaking risks.
elements.sent-2411-402: We formalize the graph generation task and establish standardized I/O formats, which are treated as prompt for LLM processing, as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-403: Now you have a graph generation task in a limited resources scenario where you don't know the distribution of the real graphs.
elements.sent-2411-404: Please just use the number of nodes, edges and the maximum node degree to generate graphs that matches the real ones as closely as possible.
elements.sent-2411-405: The input is multiple triples, each of the format: $(N, M, d)$, where $N$ and $M$ are the number of nodes and edges, $d$ is the maximum node degree.
elements.sent-2411-406: Output a list for each triple in the form of edge list.
elements.sent-2411-407: We conduct experiments on both ChatGPT and DeepSeek and select the best results to report.
